coredata.dc:title: g-C3N4 nanosheets decorated with carbon dots and CdS nanoparticles: Novel nanocomposites with excellent nitrogen photofixation ability under simulated solar irradiation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Diarmand-Khalilabad, Hadi
Habibi-Yangjeh, Aziz
Seifzadeh, Davod
Asadzadeh-Khaneghah, Soheila
Vesali-Kermani, Elham
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic nitrogen fixation, as one of the best alternatives to Haber-Bosch process, is a low–cost, eco-friendly, and mild technology that needs more efforts to find efficient photocatalysts with high stability. In this research, novel graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets/carbon dots/CdS (denoted as CN-NS/CDs/CdS) nanocomposites by prominent nitrogen photofixation capability under simulated solar irradiation were fabricated with decoration of CDs and CdS nanoparticles over CN-NS thorough a microwave-assisted method. The prepared ternary photocatalysts were investigated by XRD, EDX, SEM, TEM, HRTEM, UV–vis DRS, PL, FT-IR, TGA, BET, and EIS analyses. The results showed that photocatalytic activity of the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) nanocomposite was 17.1, 5.64, 5.38, and 4.51-folds greater than those of the bulk CN, CN-NS, pure CdS, and CN-NS/CDs samples, respectively. Moreover, an insignificant loss in the activity of photocatalyst after several cycles displayed its high stability, which is vital for the sustainable photocatalytic procedures. The effects of CdS loading, pH of solution, electron scavenger, and solvent on the ammonia generation rate were also investigated and discussed. Eventually, a probable mechanism for the significantly enhancement of N2 photofixation performance was proposed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: g-C3N4 nanosheets/carbon dots/CdS
Nitrogen fixation
Photocatalyst
Nitrogen photofixation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns for the CN, CN-NS, CN-NS/CDs, and CN-NS/CDs/CdS nanocomposites (Note that the diffraction planes indexed in parentheses are belonged to the cubic phase of CdS).
(a) EDX spectra for the CN, CN-NS, CN-NS/CDs, and CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) samples, (b-g) EDX mapping of the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) nanocomposite.
(a) SEM, (b) TEM, and (c) HRTEM images of the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) nanocomposite.
XPS spectra for the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) nanocomposite: (a) survey scan, and.
high-resolution spectra for: (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s, (d) N 1s, (e) Cd 3d, and (f) S 2p.

FT-IR spectra for the (a) CN, CN-NS, CN-NS/CDs, and CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) nanocomposites. (b) Expansion of CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) spectrum replotted in limited wavenumber range for viewing peaks of Cd-S and C-S bonds.
(a) UV–vis DR spectra, and (b) digital photographs for the CN, CN-NS, CN-NS/CDs, and CN-NS/CDs/CdS nanocomposites. (c) Plots of (αhν)2 versus hν for the CN, CN-NS, and CdS samples.
TG analyses of the CN, CN-NS, CN-NS/CDs, and CN-NS/CDs/CdS nanocomposites.

Photocatalytic NH4 + production ability of (a) different photocatalysts after irradiation for 3 h, and (b) optimal ternary nanocomposite in compared with the CN, CN-NS, CdS, and CN-NS/CDs photocatalysts. (c) Photographs of samples withdrawn successively at 30 min intervals during N2 photofixation tests over the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) nanocomposite, following reaction with Nessler's reagent.
(a) PL spectra for the CN, CN-NS, CN-NS/CDs, and CN-NS/CDs/CdS nanocomposites. (b) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption data for the CN, CN-NS, CN-NS/CDs, and CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) samples.
EIS Nyquist plots for the as-prepared photocatalysts.
(a) Mott–Schottky plots for the CN, CN-NS, and CdS. (b) Schematic diagram of the separation and transfer of photogenerated electron-hole pairs over the CN-NS/CDs/CdS nanocomposites.

NH4 + production ability of the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) (a) using KBrO3 as electron scavenger, (b) in water and aprotic solvents (DMF and DMSO), and (c) under different pH conditions after irradiation for 3 h.
Catalyst cycling tests for the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) nanocomposite.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation performance and (b) MB degradation rate constants, in the absence of the photocatalysts (photolysis), and over the CN, CdS, CN-NS, CN-NS/CDs, and CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) nanocomposites.
Schematic illustration for preparation of the CN-NS/CDs/CdS nanocomposites.
The textural properties of the CN, CN-NS, CN-NS/CDs, and CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) nanocomposite.

head.ce:title: g-C3N4 nanosheets decorated with carbon dots and CdS nanoparticles: Novel nanocomposites with excellent nitrogen photofixation ability under simulated solar irradiation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, P.O. Box 179, Ardabil, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic nitrogen fixation, as one of the best alternatives to Haber-Bosch process, is a low–cost, eco-friendly, and mild technology that needs more efforts to find efficient photocatalysts with high stability. In this research, novel graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets/carbon dots/CdS (denoted as CN-NS/CDs/CdS) nanocomposites by prominent nitrogen photofixation capability under simulated solar irradiation were fabricated with decoration of CDs and CdS nanoparticles over CN-NS thorough a microwave-assisted method. The prepared ternary photocatalysts were investigated by XRD, EDX, SEM, TEM, HRTEM, UV–vis DRS, PL, FT-IR, TGA, BET, and EIS analyses. The results showed that photocatalytic activity of the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) nanocomposite was 17.1, 5.64, 5.38, and 4.51-folds greater than those of the bulk CN, CN-NS, pure CdS, and CN-NS/CDs samples, respectively. Moreover, an insignificant loss in the activity of photocatalyst after several cycles displayed its high stability, which is vital for the sustainable photocatalytic procedures. The effects of CdS loading, pH of solution, electron scavenger, and solvent on the ammonia generation rate were also investigated and discussed. Eventually, a probable mechanism for the significantly enhancement of N2 photofixation performance was proposed.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen, as a main element in the structure of biomolecules such as proteins and nucleic acids, is an essential building block of our life. Although the major component of the atmosphere is nitrogen (~ 78%), most organisms can not directly use the molecular form of this element, due to its high N≡N bond energy. Therefore, N2 fixation to ammonia (NH3) is the most prominent chemical process in the world after photosynthesis [1,2]. This compound is widely used as chemical raw material, refrigerant, and fertilizer. The well-known Haber-Bosch process is artificial N2 fixation technology producing 500 million tons of nitrogen fertilizer per year [3–5]. In this process, NH3 is produced from the reaction of H2 and N2 gases with iron-based catalysts under high temperatures and pressures. Therefore, artificial N2 fixation under mild conditions using inexpensive materials is of remarkable importance from the environmental and economical viewpoints. Photocatalytic N2 fixation, owing to its moderate conditions, environmental benign, and low energy consumption, is one of the best alternatives to traditional industrial processes [6,7]. The first study on photocatalytic nitrogen fixation was reported by Schrauzer et al. in 1977 using Fe-doped TiO2 under UV light [8]. Since then, many photocatalysts, such as bismuth oxyhalides, polymetallic sulfides, metal oxide-based materials, and metal loaded composites have been successively reported [9–22]. Nitrogen photofixation is more challenging than photocatalytic CO2 reduction and hydrogen evolution, because the process is drastically hindered with the high-energy nitrogen intermediates in the protonated or reduced forms [23]. Accordingly, designing novel photocatalysts is momentous and a great challenge to improve and develop the nitrogen photofixation technology.

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4, abbreviated as CN) has been extremely utilized in photocatalytic processes such as degradation of pollutants [24], water splitting [25], organic synthesis [26], and CO2 capture [27], since the report of Wang et al. [25]. Dong et al. applied CN in the photocatalytic N2 fixation process for the first time, in which the ammonia generation rate was about 160 μmol L−1 h−1 g−1 [3]. Despite of appealing characteristics of moderate band gap, high stability, and cheap price, poor absorption in visible region, fast recombination of the photogenerated charges, and low surface area of about 10 m2 g−1, are usual shortcomings of bare CN [28]. Recently, many efforts have been made for modifying CN, including fabrication of its nanostructures, elemental doping, and building heterojunction with different semiconductors [29–35].

In these regards, at the present work, nanosheets of g-C3N4 (shortened as CN-NS), due to its high specific surface and good photocatalytic activity were prepared via a facile heating method [36–38]. Owing to narrow band gap of CdS and well adjustment of its energy levels with CN, we integrated them to each other to enhance visible-light absorption ability and improve interfacial migration of the photoinduced charges [39–41]. Besides, carbon dots (CDs) were used, as an electron mediator, to further reduce recombination rate of the charge carriers and gain high-performance CN-based photocatalyst. In addition, CDs show upconversion properties [42]. When CDs are coupled with other semiconductors as light absorbers, the fabricated composites could absorb longer wavelengths and emit shorter wavelengths in visible range. As a result, the photocatalysts could produce more charge carriers, and thus improve the photocatalytic efficiency. Consequently, we report a fast and facile microwave-assisted procedure for the fabrication of novel CN-NS/CDs/CdS nanocomposites, as highly efficient photocatalysts for nitrogen photofixation under simulated solar irradiation. We believe that this is the first report for fabrication of ternary CN-NS/CDs/CdS nanocomposites and their utilization in nitrogen photofixation. The prepared catalysts were characterized in detail by XRD, EDX, TEM, HRTEM, SEM, XPS, UV–vis DRS, PL, FT-IR, TGA, XPS, EIS, and BET analyses. Interestingly, the CN-NS/CDs/CdS nanocomposites showed superior photocatalytic performance both in nitrogen photofixation and in degradation of MB in comparison with the bulk CN. Effects of CdS loading, pH of the reaction medium, electron scavenger, and type of solvent on the activity were investigated. Furthermore, the photostability of the ternary sample was evaluated and eventually a possible mechanism for enhancing photocatalytic performance was offered.
The detailed characterization instruments are in the Supporting materials.

The CN, CN-NS, and CN-NS/CDs photocatalysts were prepared according to the previous work [43]. For synthesizing 0.5 g of the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) photocatalyst, where 30% is wt% of CdS, 0.35 g of the CN-NS/CDs photocatalyst was ultrasonically dispersed for 5 min in 150 mL of water. Then, 0.277 g cadmium acetate dehydrate (Cd(CH3COO)2·2H2O, Merck, 99%) was introduced to the above suspension and stirred for 60 min. Afterward, 20 mL of aqueous solution containing 0.156 g of thioacetamide (CH3CSNH2, Loba Chemie, 98.5%) was dropwisely added to the beaker under stirring for 60 min. The resultant pale goldenrod suspension was irradiated in a 750 W microwave oven for 5 min. Finally, the obtained orange suspension was gathered by centrifugation, washed two times with water and ethyl alcohol, and dried at 60 °C overnight. The ternary nanocomposites were marked as CN-NS/CDs/CdS (10%), CN-NS/CDs/CdS (20%), CN-NS/CDs/CdS (25%), CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%), CN-NS/CDs/CdS (35%), and CN-NS/CDs/CdS (40%) for the CdS amounts of 10%, 20%, 25%, 30%, 35%, and 40%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nitrogen photofixation under simulated solar irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: The N2 photofixation performances were accomplished in a cylindrical double-walled glassy photoreactor. For these tests, 50 mg of the photocatalyst was introduced to 50 mL of water. Then, 50 µL absolute ethanol (0.789 g/L) was added to the suspension, as a hole scavenger [44] and dispersed with sonication in an ultrasound bath (PARSONIC 7500 s) for 6 min. Before the irradiation, the above mixture was ceaselessly stirred in the dark for one hour with air bubbled to establish N2 adsorption balance. A 500W Xe lamp (Ushio Xenon Short Arc Lamp) was utilized as the source. The power density over the sample was 100 mW/cm2 [45]. The temperature of photoreactor was kept at 25 °C, which was supplied by a cooling water flow. After that, 1.5 mL of the prepared suspension was gathered at 30 min time intervals, diluted with water and centrifuged to precipitate the solid catalyst and separate from the liquid sample. The ammonia concentration was determined using the Nesslerization method (see Supporting materials) [9].

Methylene blue degradation tests were explored in a 500 mL cylindrical double-walled glassy reactor equipped with a water circulation arrangement (Huber CC 230) to keep the temperature at 25 °C. A 50 W LED lamp, fitted on the top of the reactor, was applied as visible-light source. Before the irradiation, the suspension containing 250 mL of the pollutant solution (1 × 10–5 M) and 100 mg of the photocatalyst was stirred and unceasingly aerated for one hour in dark to attain equilibrium. During the tests, the specific portion of above suspension was withdrawn at given interval times and then centrifuged to eliminate photocatalyst particles before spectrophotometric analysis at 664 nm.

The XRD measurements were used to display the crystalline structure of the photocatalysts ( Fig. 1). For the pure CN and CN-NS powders, two peaks at 13.5° and 27.5° are corresponding to the (100) and (002) planes, respectively (JCPDS no. 87-1526) [46]. The same pattern was observed for the CN-NS/CDs sample, demonstrating that by adding CDs, no change occurred in the structure of the CN-NS and no extra peaks were detected, because of limited quantity of CDs in the samples [47]. For the CN-NS/CDs/CdS photocatalysts, intensity of the characteristic peaks of CN-NS decreased in the lowest weight percentage of CdS and disappeared in higher amounts of it and the peaks at 25.57°, 26.77°, 28.37°, 44.02°, and 52.27° in the patterns of CN-NS/CDs/CdS were attributed to the hexagonal and cubic phases of CdS (JCPDS card files no. 80-0006 and 75-0581, respectively), suggesting that CdS nanoparticles have been favorably anchored over the surface of CN-NS [48,49]. It is obvious that intensity of the XRD peaks for CdS gradually strengthen by increasing weight percentage of CdS. Hence, it is inferred that the nanocomposites have been synthesized and no additional peaks related to the probable impurities were identified, confirming that the photocatalysts have high purity.

Fig. 2a shows the EDX spectra for the pure, binary, and ternary nanocomposites. The spectra for the CN and CN-NS samples show peaks corresponding to the presence of carbon and nitrogen elements, while the spectrum of the binary CN-NS/CDs composite mainly contains C, N, and O elements, owing to the presence of different oxygen-containing groups in CDs [50]. The peaks in the EDX spectrum of the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) photocatalyst are attributed to the C, N, O, Cd, and S elements. Therefore, the ternary nanocomposite has been favorably prepared through the proposed facile method. Fig. 2(b–g) show the distribution of elements in the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) photocatalyst, conforming that all of the five elements presented in the ternary nanocomposite are obviously seen.

The microstructure and morphology of the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) nanocomposite were explored using SEM, TEM, and HRTEM images, which are shown in Fig. 3. In the SEM and TEM images, deposited nanoparticles are clearly seen over the CN-NS sheets. To observe small particles of CDs, HRTEM image of the ternary photocatalyst was presented in Fig. 3c. As seen, the interplanar spacings of 0.325 nm [51] and 0.320 nm [47] are related to the (002) plane of CN-NS and the (002) plane of CDs. Lattice fringes of 0.176 nm [52] and 0.336 nm [53] are ascribed to the (311) plane of cubic CdS and (002) plane of hexagonal CdS, respectively.

XPS analysis was employed to precisely confirm the chemical status of elements in the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) photocatalyst, as the spectra shown in Fig. 4. Fig. 4a shows the survey spectrum, which contains C, N, O, Cd, and S elements presented in the photocatalyst. In addition, no diagnostic peaks of the other elements were observed, verifying purity of the nanocomposite. As observed in Fig. 4b, the C 1s XPS spectrum shows three obvious peaks at 284.48, 285.58, and 288.02 eV, which are belonged to sp2 C-C, sp3 C-NH2 bonds, and sp2 bonded carbon in the N-containing aromatic rings (N-C˭N) of CN-NS, respectively. Also, the peak located in the C 1s XPS spectrum at 285.58 eV is contributed to C-OH group, revealing the existence of CDs and significant interaction between CN-NS and CDs (Fig. 4b) [54–57]. The O 1s spectrum shows three peaks located at 531.14, 532.43, and 533.65 eV, which are ascribed to the C–O–C/C–OH, C˭O, and H–O–H groups, respectively (Fig. 4c) [56]. The XPS spectrum of N 1s exhibits three characteristic peaks located at 398.47, 399.45, and 404.77 eV, respectively. The peaks centered at 398.47 and 399.45 eV are ascribed to sp2-hybridized C-N=C and N-(C)3 groups presented in the CN-NS structure (Fig. 4d). The strong peak at 404.77 eV is ascribed to terminal nitrate groups, charging effects, or π excitations [57]. The high resolution Cd 3d XPS spectrum of the nanocomposite (Fig. 4e) shows the peaks of Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2 located at 404.76 and 411.50 eV, corresponding to Cd2+ ions in the CdS nanoparticles [57,58]. Fig. 4f reveals the XPS signals of S 2p observed at 161.07 and 162.26 eV, which are related to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively, as anticipated for the S2− in CdS nanoparticles. It is noteworthy that the small peak around 167.59 eV can be attributed to the S-C bond, indicating the interaction between CdS and CN-NS [57,58].

FT-IR analysis was applied to characterize functional groups of the CN, CN-NS, CN-NS/CDs, and CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) samples, as shown in Fig. 5. The broad peak regarding the spectrum of bulk CN at 3000–3800 cm−1 is related to O-H and N-H stretching vibrations [59]. Several characteristic peaks located in the range of 1240–1650 cm−1 with peaks centered at 1240, 1322, 1423, 1572, and 1636 cm−1 imply the distinctive stretching modes of aromatic C-N heterocycles [60]. The sharp peak at 810 cm−1 is corresponded to the breathing mode of the heptazine units [60,61]. Due to the presence of unoxidized hydrocarbons, carbonyl, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups in CDs, the nanocomposites with CD nanoparticles exhibit the peaks at 1320 and 1638 cm−1, which are related to the C-H bending and C˭O stretching vibrations, respectively [62]. In the spectrum of the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%), the bands located at 1050 and 690 cm−1 can be ascribed to the Cd–S, and the peaks centered at 1550 cm−1 and 1180 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of C-S bonds (as clearly seen in Fig. 5b) [63–65]. Also, a peak located at 2374 cm−1 can be seen in all catalysts, which is belonged to atmospheric CO2 [66]. Furthermore, the results approved that the C–N ring structure of graphite samples was not changed by exfoliation and depositing particles of CDs and CdS.

Optical behavior of the prepared photocatalysts was investigated using UV–vis DR spectra. These spectra for the CN, CN-NS, CN-NS/CDs, and CN-NS/CDs/CdS with various compositions were recorded and the results are displayed in Fig. 6. The absorption edge of bulk CN located at nearly 490 nm, which is attributed to the n→π* electronic transitions of nitrogen lone pairs [67]. In comparison with the pure CN, the CN-NS shows a blue shift, verifying ultrathin characteristic of the CN-NS sample [47]. The CN-NS/CDs photocatalyst has higher visible absorption in comparison with the bulk CN, due to integration of CDs with the nanosheets of CN [42]. On the other hand, because of integration of CdS (E
g = 2.4 eV) with CN (E
g = 2.7 eV), a clear red shift in the absorption edge of the CN-NS/CDs/CdS composites is also seen. Thus, the fabricated CN-NS/CDs/CdS nanocomposites might be favorable for photoactivity under visible light. Also, the enhanced absorption of visible light with an increase in the CdS weight percentage is seen in the color change of the as-prepared photocatalysts, turning from pale goldenrod to dark orange (Fig. 6b). To estimate the band gap energy (E
g ) of the fabricated photocatalysts, the Tauc's equation was used. The E
g values were calculated by extrapolating the linear parts of (αhυ)2 versus hυ curves. As observed in Fig. 6c, values of E
g are 2.70, 2.73, and 2.40 eV for the CN, CN-NS, and CdS samples, respectively.

In the next section, thermal stability of the prepared nanocomposites was studied by TGA from 20 °C to 700 °C. For the bulk CN, CN-NS, CN-NS/CDs, and CN-NS/CDs/CdS photocatalysts with various amounts of CdS, a two-step weight decrease is seen. Small weight loss up to 140 °C is because of the adsorbed H2O evaporation from the photocatalysts, and the considerable weight losses in the range of 400–680 °C are attributed to the combustion of CN parts of the mentioned photocatalysts [43]. As observed in Fig. 7, decomposition of the CN counterpart starts approximately from 490 °C and completed at 620 °C. Nevertheless, converting CN to the CN-NS and combining with CDs and CdS, decreased the thermostability [68]. Finally, weight percentages (wt%) of organic parts in the nanocomposites were calculated and they are 90.2, 80.1, 75.6, 70.6, 65.1, and 60.2 wt% for the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (10%), CN-NS/CDs/CdS (20%), CN-NS/CDs/CdS (25%), CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%), CN-NS/CDs/CdS (35%), and CN-NS/CDs/CdS (40%) photocatalysts, respectively.

Fig. 8a displays the photocatalytic N2 fixation ability over the fabricated samples under the light irradiation for 3 h. This figure shows that the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) composite exhibits extreme photocatalytic ability compared with the other photocatalysts. It is noteworthy that the photocatalytic activity of the CN-NS/CDs/CdS catalysts at higher than 30% of CdS (particularly in 40%), decreased. This decrease may be attributed to formation of a dense shell of CdS over the CN-NS/CDs sheets, which prevents the heterojunction formation between the counterparts. Furthermore, as observed in Fig. 8b, the concentrations of the produced NH4 + over the CN-NS and CN-NS/CDs samples are 5.63 × 102, and 7.04 × 102 μmol L−1 g−1, respectively, which are 3.03 and 3.79-folds premier than that of the bulk CN (1.86 × 102 μmol L−1 g−1). In order to evaluate the nitrogen photofixation ability of the pure CdS, activity of this semiconductor was also investigated and compared with other photocatalysts. As seen in Fig. 8a, pure CdS has low activity (5.90 × 102 μmol L−1 g−1) rather than the binary and ternary photocatalysts. The nitrogen fixation ability of the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) nanocomposite was 3.18 × 103 μmol L−1 g−1, which was 17.1 and 5.38 times premier than that of the bulk CN and pure CdS, respectively (Fig. 8b). Consequently, combination of CN-NS with CDs and CdS has considerable influence on the nitrogen photofixation activity. Fig. 8c shows the photographs of the samples withdrawn successively within 30 min intervals during the light irradiation over the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) nanocomposite after adding Nessler's reagent. As seen, the green color of the solution is intensified with the irradiation time.

Fig. 9a shows PL emission spectra for the bulk CN, CN-NS, CN-NS/CDs, and CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) samples to prospect separation efficiency of the charges. As observed, the highest PL spectrum intensity belongs to the CN, while the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) nanocomposite has the lowest emission intensity. Thus, the results indicate that recombination rate of the charges in the ternary nanocomposite is minimum in comparison with the other samples, which matches well with their photocatalytic abilities.

Textural properties of the prepared photocatalysts were determined by nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms according to the BET analyses and the results are seen in Fig. 9b and Table 1. The BET specific surface areas (SBET) of the CN, CN-NS, CN-NS/CDs, and CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) samples were calculated to be 14.6, 88.1, 51.2, and 17.3 m2 g−1, respectively. As observed, magnitude of the specific surface area for the CN-NS powder, which is about 6 times premier than that of the CN, indicates high degree of exfoliation. In contrast, the CN-NS/CDs and CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) samples have considerably lower SBET than the CN-NS powder. This reduction could be attributed to blocking some of the active sites of the CN-NS by deposited CDs and CdS nanoparticles [69]. Therefore, the high photocatalytic performance of the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) photocatalyst compared with the other prepared samples is not related to SBET.

Fig. 10 shows the Nyquist plots of EIS data, which were applied to verify the interfacial charge transfer characteristics of the fabricated samples. In general, the arc radius in the Nyquist plots of EIS represents the reaction rate over the working electrode [70]. The small arc radius reflects a low resistance electrode, which corresponded to impressive separation of e-/h+ pairs and supplying enough electrons to the nitrogen fixation reaction [70,71]. As seen in Fig. 13, the CN and CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) samples exhibit the largest and smallest arc radiuses among the prepared photocatalysts, respectively. According to the mentioned information, the ternary nanocomposite with 30 wt% of CdS has lowest resistance for the charge transfer, revealing more effectual separation of the photoinduced charges, which is useful for improving the photocatalytic performance.

In order to propose a mechanism for the N2 photofixation over the ternary nanocomposites, the band positions of the samples were estimated by the Mott–Schottky plots, as shown in Fig. 11a. The positive slope of these plots indicates that the CN, CN-NS, and CdS samples are n-type semiconductors [72]. The flat-band potentials (E
fb ) of these samples were assessed from extrapolation to the x intercept in the Mott–Schottky plots [73]. Hence, the E
fb of the CN, CN-NS and CdS are about − 1.24, − 1.36, and − 0.61 V vs. Ag/AgCl, respectively (− 1.03,− 1.15, and − 0.40 V vs NHE). As know, for an n-type semiconductor, the bottom of its CB is negative than E
fb and in many of them, the bottom of the E
CB is more negative by ~ -0.1 V than the E
fb
[74]. Therefore, E
CB of the CN, CN-NS, and CdS can be calculated to − 1.13, − 1.25, and − 0.50 eV, respectively. The VB for the samples can be calculated using the E
g and the E
CB values, using Eq. (1):
(1)
E
V B
=
E
C B
+
E
g

Thereupon, the E
VB of CN, CN-NS, and CdS were calculated to be 1.57, 1.48, and 1.90 eV, respectively. According to the above mentioned results, the possible mechanism for the enhanced activity of nitrogen photofixation reaction over the CN-NS/CDs/CdS photocatalysts is offered, as observed in Fig. 11b. Theoretically, the CB positions for CN-NS and CdS are negative than the redox potential of N2/NH3 (-0.0922 V vs. NHE) [75]. Hence, both semiconductors can reduce N2 to NH3. It is noteworthy that upon the simulated solar irradiation, both the CN-NS and CdS are excited to generate electrons and holes. Because of the suitable overlapping band structures and strictly contacted interfaces of the semiconductors, the photogenerated electrons desire to migrate from CN-NS to CdS driven by the CB offset of 0.75 eV, while the holes spontaneously move from CdS to CN-NS driven by the VB offset of 0.42 eV. Additionally, when CDs are deposited onto the surface of CN-NS, the integrated CDs play the following roles in the photocatalytic process. Firstly, transfer of the photoinduced electrons from CN-NS to CDs is thermodynamically favorable, which makes electrons to simply transfer from CN-NS to CDs. Secondly, CDs can act as electron transfer mediator to promote the electron transfer from CN-NS to CdS, which improves the separation of electron-hole pairs. In addition, CDs possess upconversion photoluminescence properties. Hence, they absorb longer wavelengths from visible light (λ > 550 nm) and then emit shorter wavelengths (λ < 460 nm), leading to exciting CN-NS to form more charge carriers, which can also contribute in the enhanced photocatalytic activity of CN-NS/CDs/CdS [42]. Meanwhile, the electrons on the CB of CdS immediately transfer to the antibonding orbitals of adsorbed N2 molecules [76]. Afterwards, the nitrogen molecules gain successively H+ ions from the environment and the photogenerated electrons to form ammonia (NH3), and thereupon it eventually forms NH4 + [76].

Fig. 12 provides preliminary analyses of the nitrogen photofixation mechanism over the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) nanocomposite. As observed in Fig. 12a, the addition of KBrO3 (as an electron scavenger) obviously suppresses the N2 photofixation activity of the ternary nanocomposite. On the other hand, the NH4 + generation rate becomes very low when DMSO and DMF were used as aprotic solvents instead of water (Fig. 12b). These results demonstrate that electrons are main active species for nitrogen reduction reaction and water provides protons (see Eq. (2)). To further investigate the latest issue, the effect of solution pH on the ammonia concentration over the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) nanocomposite was studied. As observed in Fig. 12c, by decrease of solution pH, the NH4 + concentration was increased from 3.72 × 103 µmol L−1 g−1 (at pH 8) to 5.81 × 103 µmol L−1 g−1 (at pH 3). This is due to availability of excess protons to increase the reaction rate in acidic media [77]. The reactions are shown in Eqs. (2) and (3) [78]:
(2) N2 + 6H+ + 6e−→ 2NH3
(3) NH3 + H2O → NH3·H2O ⇌ NH4 + + OH−
However, with further decrease of the solution pH to 2, the generation of NH4 + was decreased, which can be attributed to the decrease of photocatalyst stability in more acidic media.

Stability of the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) photocatalyst for N2 photofixation was investigated in a recycling test with four cycles. As shown in Fig. 13, no considerable decrease in photocatalytic activity is observed, hinting good stability of this ternary photocatalyst.

The photocatalytic degradation performance of MB upon the fabricated nanocomposites was also investigated under visible-light irradiation ( Fig. 14a). The reaction rate constant was earned by supposing that the reaction pursued pseudo-first-order kinetics (inset of Figs. 14a and 14b) [79]. As clearly shown, a blank test (in the absence of the photocatalysts) shows that the photolysis of MB is negligible. Similar to the nitrogen photofixation, the CN, CN-NS, and CN-NS/CDs photocatalysts displayed also low activities in the degradation reaction. Additionally, for comparison, photocatalytic activity of the pure CdS was investigated. As seen in Fig. 14b, the rate constants are about 8.95 × 10−4, 9.68 × 10−4, 1.81 × 10−3, and 3.72 × 10−3 min−1 for the CN, CdS, CN-NS, and CN-NS/CDs photocatalysts, respectively. The ternary nanocomposite exhibited the highest rate constant of 4.48 × 10−2 min−1, which is about 50, 46.3, 25, and 12-folds premier than those of the CN, CdS, CN-NS, and CN-NS/CDs samples. As observed, the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) nanocomposite displayed substantially improved photocatalytic performance in comparison with the other photocatalysts. As a consequence, the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) nanocomposite has remarkable potential to be used in photofixation reaction and removal of pollutants from wastewaters ( Scheme 1).

In this work, novel ternary CN-NS/CDs/CdS photocatalysts were prepared with fast and simple microwave-assisted method and utilized for the nitrogen photofixation reaction under simulated solar irradiation. The 30 wt% CdS modified CN-NS/CDs showed the maximum NH4 + generation rate, which was 17.1, 5.64, 5.38, and 4.51-folds premier than those of the CN, CN-NS, pure CdS, and CN-NS/CDs samples, respectively. The synergistic influences of CN-NS, CDs, and CdS collaborated in impressively enhancing solar-light absorption, reducing recombination of the charge carriers, and increasing mobility of electrons, which are beneficial in improving the photocatalytic activity. Moreover, the mechanistic investigations indicated that the generation rate of NH4 + by the ternary nanocomposite is almost related to the presence of electrons and protons, as the main reactive species in the nitrogen reduction reactions. Finally, it was observed that the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) sample had good photocatalytic stability during the consecutive four cycles. Additionally, the ternary nanocomposite exhibited the highest rate constant of 4.48 × 10−2 min−1 in MB degradation reaction, which was about 50, 46.3, 25, and 12-folds premier than those of the CN, pure CdS, CN-NS, and CN-NS/CDs samples. As a consequence, the CN-NS/CDs/CdS (30%) nanocomposite has remarkable potential to be used in photofixation reaction and removal of pollutants from wastewaters.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledged the University of Mohaghegh Ardabili-Iran for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.ceramint.2018.10.185.
